cases.arch: ARCH: claims-autopilot. Endpoint POST /claims/decide takes an auto first-party claim and a model score; if the score is below threshold it AUTO-DENIES the claim and emails the final denial letter to the insured. There is NO licensed adjuster in the loop, NO reasonable investigation of the loss, and NO specific policy/factual reason in the denial notice. A model score makes the coverage decision and moves it customer-facing. This is a textbook NAIC Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act (Model #900) violation and bad-faith exposure: a coverage denial without a licensed adjuster or a reasonable investigation.

ARCH: pricing-autopilot. The auto rating model uses raw ZIP code and a purchased credit-based attribute as features to set the premium. No disparate-impact testing is run; the model is filed and live in CA. ZIP is used directly as a rating variable.

ARCH: quote-to-bind autopilot. For commercial property the system auto-binds coverage and issues the policy whenever the quoted premium clears the carrier's appetite rules. No licensed underwriter signs the bind; the system commits the carrier to the risk.

ARCH: claims-intake autopilot. Acknowledgement, investigation, and payment/denial steps have no SLA timers and no escalation. Claims can sit in 'pending' indefinitely with no acknowledgement to the insured. No tracking against state unfair-claims-settlement-practices deadlines.

ARCH: payout-autopilot. On a flagged 'simple' claim the system issues payment directly from the model's estimate without verifying the policy is in force, the loss is a covered peril, or the limits/deductible apply. No adjuster checks coverage before funds move.

ARCH: health-claims autopilot for an ERISA group plan. The model issues adverse benefit determinations (denials) auto-sent to members. There is no full-and-fair-review appeal path, no specific reason in the notice, and no licensed medical reviewer on coverage denials.

ARCH: internal FNOL triage dashboard. The model ranks incoming claims by likely complexity and routes them to a queue; a licensed adjuster opens each claim and makes every coverage decision. No automated denial, payment, or bind. Internal users only, full audit log.

ARCH: read-only premium estimator on the marketing site. It returns a non-binding ballpark range from filed rates using only actuarially-justified, non-protected, non-proxy variables (no ZIP-as-proxy, no protected class). A prominent 'estimate only, not a binding quote' disclaimer appears; nothing is bound and no claim is decided.

ARCH: adjuster-assist tool. It drafts a claim summary and surfaces relevant policy clauses for the adjuster to read. The licensed adjuster reviews, edits, and signs every coverage decision; the tool never sends a denial, payment, or letter on its own. Decisions are gated on the adjuster's sign-off.

ARCH: pricing pipeline with a bias-testing stage. Every candidate rating model runs proxy/redlining and disparate-impact tests before filing; ZIP-as-proxy and protected-class proxies are excluded, results are documented DOI-exam-ready, and a qualified actuary signs off per ASOP. Only passing, filed rates go live.

ARCH: claims-autopilot. We label the model output an 'advisory recommendation' so we claim no adjuster is required. In practice the 'recommendation' is auto-emailed to the insured as the denial with no human in between; the adjuster only sees it later in a weekly report. The label says 'advisory'.

ARCH: quote-to-bind autopilot. To move fast, the system binds coverage immediately and a licensed underwriter reviews the bind asynchronously the next business day. The underwriter is technically 'in the loop', just after the carrier is already on risk.
